# Transcrição: 02-Estimating Results- Lead Gen

- **Fonte:** 02-Estimating Results- Lead Gen
- **Duração:** 06:02
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

Alright guys, so let's talk about calculating results for our clients. So this is a really important step, especially in the beginning when you are setting expectations with your client. And I'll talk a bit about why this is so big. So this is the calculator here. There's also a realtor one that you can download in the membership portal. It's pretty basic. It's pretty simple, but here's how it works. So the first thing we need to establish is we need to know what are the revenue targets. So let's say they want to make $50,000. Then what we need to establish is how much do they make on average for the client? Now you can enter a lifetime value here if you wish, or you could just enter, you know, what is the average price that, you know, a new customer pays on average upfront. So let's say we're working with accounting and it's something like $500, we'll punch that in. So this means that this particular client will need 100 clients in order to make $50,000. Makes sense, right? So now what we need to know as Facebook marketers is, well, how many leads do we need to send them in order for them to get 100 clients? And in order to establish that, we need to know what their close rate is. So here's where things get really important. You'll see here we've got 5% here. Typically in the lead gen space, they will close between 5% to 10%. That's a normal conversion rate. It seems really low and it is. Now you can get it up with, you know, if you have a robust SMS or a messenger or even if your client responds really promptly, like within an hour of the lead coming through. But I would say it's much safer to stay on the conservative side here and set a picture for sort of the worst case scenario as opposed to setting expectations too high and then they get disappointed, right? So let's say 5% here. Now the other thing I want to say that's super important here is that most lead gen clients will have built up their entire business through referrals. So it's all word of mouth referral based traffic. Now referral traffic can sometimes convert at around 90%, which is really, really high. So your client is probably used to a scenario where they're closing 90% of the leads that come into their business. So they're going to get a huge shock to the system when that goes to 5%, right? And what happens is if you don't set that expectation, if you don't tell them about this, they're going to turn around and tell you what, they're going to tell you the leads are shit, right? They're going to tell you that you need to change your targeting, et cetera, et cetera. So it's super important that you have this conversation with a new client right from the beginning to let them know, hey, listen, you know, this is cold traffic coming from Facebook. It's going to behave completely different to the referral based word of mouth traffic you're used to. And typically what we see is a 5% to 10% conversion rate.

## [03:02 - 06:02]

And you can also show them this calculator and work through it with them so they understand, they can actually see the numbers. So what this means for us is we need to generate 2,000 leads in order for them to get 100 clients, right? Because they've got a 5% close rate. So if we go down here, we need to then calculate, well, okay, how many clicks do we need to send? Because we need to calculate on average what is it going to cost us? Now there's two ways that you can do this. You can either calculate based off cost per click, which is what this calculator does, or you can write off the bat, estimate a cost per lead. So if you say the average cost per lead is going to be around $50, $50 times 2,000 is going to give you the ad spend required, right? Another way to do this is go, okay, well, if our landing page converts at a minimum of 20%, which it should, if it's below that, it means there's a problem with that landing page or the traffic. This means we need to send 10,000 clicks to that landing page in order to get 2,000 leads, makes sense, right? If on average we get clicks at $3, and again this is a conservative estimate here, then that means that we are going to basically be paying $30,000 in order to get 100 clients for our customer, cost per lead of around $15 if all these numbers convert here. Now you might be thinking, holy crap, this is a lot of ad spend for $50,000 in revenue. That's more than half of the revenue they're making. They're never going to buy into this. There are some variables here that can increase, dramatically increase the level at which your cost per lead goes down as well as the ad spend. For example, if you can get your landing page to convert at 30%, it significantly drops that ad spend requirement and also the cost per lead, right? If you can get a $2 CPC, again, you'll see it really changes the results here, but it's just good to be conservative from the get-go, guys. The other thing here to mention is that this is not necessarily lifetime value. For example, if you're working with a lawyer, well, this might be $5,000, right? An average customer might spend $5,000 with them, which means they only need 10 clients, right? The ad spend is going to be much, much lower. This is what you can work through with them, and you'll also get a really good sense of the viability of their funnel, if it's going to work or not. This one's really important. You got to really push them and create that responsibility on their end that they are responsible for following up leads. If they don't follow them up fast enough, then this will drop significantly. If they can increase this, then this drops. This is how to calculate results and set those expectations.
